title: How to Run an Autoresearch Workflow with RL Agent Skills and NVIDIA NeMo | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA demonstrates that a coding agent (Codex with GPT 5.5), guided by specialized 'agent skills' for infrastructure hygiene, session memory, and experiment management, can autonomously run full reinforcement-learning research workflows on NVIDIA NeMo RL/NeMo Gym and Brev GPU instances — including building a new RL environment from scratch, improving a VLM's accuracy from 25% to 96.9%, and implementing a novel off-policy RL algorithm (OAPL) directly from an arXiv paper.

themes.theme: Autonomous agents as RL research operators

insights.claim: Using a goal-driven autoresearch loop, Codex (GPT 5.5) built a new NeMo Gym visual counting environment ('star count') from scratch and trained Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct via LoRA SFT, improving exact-match accuracy from a 25.0% out-of-the-box baseline to 96.875% within a 5-hour time budget, achieving the largest gains by scaling SFT data and training steps.
insights.speaker: Vinh Nguyen
insights.so_what: A 25%→96.9% accuracy jump achieved with minimal human intervention (agent designed the environment, ran the campaign, and self-selected SFT over GRPO after GRPO underperformed) is a concrete benchmark for how much ML engineering labor coding agents can now absorb in RL research pipelines.

insights.claim: Codex was tasked to read an arXiv paper describing OAPL (Optimal Advantage-based Policy Optimization with Lagged Inference policy), a long-lag off-policy RL method, then independently form an implementation plan, write and test the code, document it, and start a 10-hour validation training campaign on Qwen3-1.7B with the DAPO-math-17K dataset — a 'paper-to-code' capability pioneered conceptually by OpenAI's PaperBench.
insights.speaker: Vinh Nguyen
insights.so_what: Autonomous paper-to-code implementation of a novel RL algorithm (not just applying an existing recipe) signals coding agents are approaching capability to accelerate the research-to-production pipeline for AI labs, potentially compressing R&D headcount needs for algorithm implementation work.

insights.claim: In the OAPL validation run, the agent-implemented algorithm reached higher accuracy with fewer training steps than a GRPO variant (DAPO) on Qwen3-1.7B with DAPO-math-17K, consistent with the paper's central claim about effective long-lag off-policy training, but used significantly more tokens than DAPO — raising an open question about verbosity versus accuracy tradeoffs.
insights.speaker: Vinh Nguyen
insights.so_what: Confirms independent (agent-driven) reproduction of a paper's core claim, which is a meaningful validation signal for OAPL as a promising off-policy RL technique that labs training reasoning models may want to evaluate.
insights.locator: Step 10: Training validation and follow-up research question

insights.claim: Autoresearch is an open-source Python project created by Andrej Karpathy for automating AI/ML model training, which NVIDIA combined with three custom agent skills (Brev-etiquette for infrastructure hygiene, session-memory for durable state across long sessions, and autoresearch for the experiment loop with ledgered tracking).
insights.speaker: Vinh Nguyen
insights.so_what: Notable ecosystem convergence: NVIDIA is building production RL research tooling on top of Andrej Karpathy's open-source autoresearch framework, indicating cross-pollination between independent AI research tooling and NVIDIA's commercial NeMo stack.
insights.locator: Building an autonomous RL research workflow

insights.claim: The author identifies recurring failure modes in long-running agentic research sessions: context drift (agent forgets objective/skills/stop-rules after context compaction), local housekeeping failures (scattered logs/checkpoints without explicit machine etiquette), steering drift (mid-run human questions derailing sessions), and low-signal loops (agent stuck training with insufficient batch/steps to produce real signal).
insights.speaker: Vinh Nguyen
insights.so_what: A practical checklist of agentic-workflow failure modes that any team deploying autonomous coding agents for long-running compute jobs (not just RL research) should design safeguards against — durable memory, explicit budgets, and human spot-checks remain necessary.
insights.locator: Tips and best practices for autoresearch workflows

insights.claim: The author frames the current state of agent-led research as division of labor, not replacement: the human researcher still sets goals, provides domain judgment, steers campaigns, and makes final decisions, while explicitly recommending explicit budget constraints (time, GPU-hours, experiment count) before starting any long-running autoresearch session.
insights.speaker: Vinh Nguyen
insights.so_what: A calibrated, non-hyperbolic claim about agentic AI's current role in ML research — useful counterweight to narratives of fully autonomous AI research, and a governance practice (explicit budgets) investors should look for when evaluating AI labs' agent-driven R&D efficiency claims.
insights.quote: Agents are not replacing researchers, but rather supporting a productive division of labor.
